_comment:
Guidelines for Questions:
- Available Difficulties: Easy, Intermediate, Hard
- Available Topics: Scalability, Microservices, Databases, Caching, Load Balancing
- Make sure each question has an id, subject, topic, difficulty, title, question, answer, explanation, code_example (optional), and key_takeaways (array).

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: What is System Design?
questions.question: What is System Design? Explain its purpose, importance, key objectives, and real-world applications.
questions.answer: System Design is the process of defining the architecture, components, modules, interfaces, and data flow of a software system to satisfy specific business and technical requirements. It acts as a blueprint that guides developers in building scalable, reliable, maintainable, and efficient applications. System Design focuses on how different parts of a system interact with each other, how data is stored and processed, and how the system handles growth, failures, security, and performance requirements. Modern applications such as social media platforms, e-commerce websites, banking systems, video streaming services, and cloud platforms rely heavily on well-designed systems to serve millions of users efficiently.
questions.explanation:
Imagine constructing a large shopping mall. Before construction begins, architects create detailed blueprints that define the structure, electrical systems, water supply, emergency exits, and parking areas. Without proper planning, the building may become unsafe, inefficient, or difficult to maintain.

Similarly, before developing a software application, engineers create a system design. The design specifies how users interact with the application, how requests are processed, where data is stored, how security is implemented, and how the system scales when the number of users increases.

For example, when designing an online shopping platform such as Amazon, engineers must decide how product data will be stored, how orders will be processed, how payments will be handled, and how the platform will continue working even if some servers fail. System Design provides solutions to these challenges and ensures the application can grow successfully over time.
questions.code_example:
// High-Level Components
User -> Load Balancer -> Application Servers -> Database
                           |
                           -> Cache
key_takeaways: System Design is the blueprint of a software system.
It focuses on scalability, reliability, and maintainability.
It defines architecture, components, and data flow.
Helps engineers build efficient and fault-tolerant systems.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Functional and Non-Functional Requirements
questions.question: What are Functional Requirements and Non-Functional Requirements in System Design? Explain their differences with examples.
questions.answer: Functional Requirements define what a system should do. They describe the features, functionalities, and operations that users expect from the system. Examples include user registration, login functionality, payment processing, product search, and order placement. Non-Functional Requirements define how the system should perform while delivering those functionalities. They include scalability, reliability, performance, security, availability, maintainability, and fault tolerance. Both types of requirements are essential because functional requirements define system behavior, while non-functional requirements determine system quality and user experience.
questions.explanation:
Consider an online food delivery application.

Functional Requirements:
- Users can register and log in.
- Users can search restaurants.
- Users can place orders.
- Delivery agents can accept orders.

Non-Functional Requirements:
- The application should support 1 million users.
- Pages should load within 2 seconds.
- The system should be available 99.99% of the time.
- User data should be encrypted.

Think of building a car. Functional requirements define what the car can do, such as accelerating, braking, and turning. Non-functional requirements define how well it performs, such as fuel efficiency, speed, safety, and reliability. Both are required to create a successful product.
key_takeaways: Functional requirements define what a system does.
Non-functional requirements define how a system performs.
Both are critical for successful system design.
Scalability and performance are non-functional requirements.
Features such as login and payment are functional requirements.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: What is Scalability?
questions.question: What is Scalability in System Design? Explain its types, importance, and practical examples.
questions.answer: Scalability is the ability of a system to handle increasing workloads, users, transactions, and data volumes without significant degradation in performance. A scalable system can continue operating efficiently as demand grows. Scalability is one of the most important goals in modern system design because successful applications often experience rapid growth in traffic and usage. Engineers design scalable systems to ensure future expansion without requiring a complete redesign of the architecture.
questions.explanation:
Imagine a restaurant designed to serve 50 customers per day. If suddenly 5,000 customers arrive daily, the restaurant will fail unless it expands its seating, staff, and kitchen capacity.

Software systems face similar challenges.

For example, Instagram initially served a small number of users. As millions joined the platform, engineers had to scale databases, application servers, storage systems, and content delivery networks.

There are two primary types of scalability:

Vertical Scaling (Scale Up):
Increasing resources of a single server such as CPU, RAM, or storage.

Horizontal Scaling (Scale Out):
Adding more servers to distribute the workload.

Large internet companies prefer horizontal scaling because it provides better fault tolerance and supports massive growth.
key_takeaways: Scalability is the ability to handle growth.
Scalability is critical for large applications.
Proper scalability planning prevents future bottlenecks.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: What is Availability?
questions.question: What is Availability in System Design? Explain its importance, measurement, and techniques used to achieve high availability.
questions.answer: Availability refers to the percentage of time a system remains operational and accessible to users. A highly available system continues functioning even when individual components fail. Availability is usually measured as a percentage known as uptime. Modern internet-scale applications often target 99.9%, 99.99%, or even 99.999% availability. High availability is crucial for banking systems, healthcare applications, e-commerce platforms, and cloud services where downtime can lead to financial losses and poor user experiences.
questions.explanation:
Consider an online payment system.

If the service becomes unavailable during a major shopping event, customers cannot complete purchases and the business loses revenue.

To improve availability, engineers use:

Redundancy:
Multiple servers performing the same tasks.

Load Balancers:
Distribute traffic across multiple servers.

Failover Systems:
Automatically switch to backup systems during failures.

Replication:
Maintain multiple copies of critical data.

Monitoring:
Detect issues before users notice them.

For example, if one server crashes, a load balancer redirects users to healthy servers without interruption.
questions.code_example:
Users
  |
Load Balancer
 /      \
Server1 Server2
   |
 Database Replicas
key_takeaways: Availability is critical for business continuity.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: What is a Load Balancer?
questions.question: What is a Load Balancer? Explain its working, types, benefits, and role in modern distributed systems.
questions.answer: A Load Balancer is a system component that distributes incoming network traffic across multiple servers to ensure no single server becomes overloaded. It acts as a traffic manager positioned between users and backend servers. Load balancers improve performance, availability, fault tolerance, and scalability by efficiently utilizing available resources. They are fundamental building blocks of modern distributed systems and are commonly used in cloud computing, web applications, microservices architectures, and enterprise systems.
questions.explanation:
Imagine a supermarket with only one checkout counter. As customer traffic increases, long queues develop and service becomes slow.

If multiple checkout counters are available and customers are distributed among them, service becomes faster and more efficient.

A load balancer performs a similar role in software systems.

When users send requests:

User Requests
      ↓
 Load Balancer
   ↓   ↓   ↓
Server1 Server2 Server3

The load balancer decides which server should handle each request.

Common algorithms include:

Round Robin
Least Connections
Weighted Round Robin
IP Hashing

If one server fails, the load balancer automatically routes traffic to healthy servers, improving reliability and availability.
questions.code_example:
Client Requests
      |
Load Balancer
 /   |   \
S1   S2   S3

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: What is Caching?
questions.question: What is Caching in System Design? Explain its working, benefits, challenges, and real-world applications.
questions.answer: Caching is a technique used to store frequently accessed data in a temporary high-speed storage layer called a cache. Instead of repeatedly fetching data from slower systems such as databases or external services, applications retrieve data from the cache, significantly reducing response time and system load. Caching is one of the most effective optimization techniques used in large-scale systems because it improves performance, reduces latency, lowers infrastructure costs, and enhances user experience. Modern platforms such as Google, YouTube, Netflix, Facebook, Amazon, and Instagram heavily rely on caching to serve billions of requests efficiently.
questions.explanation:
Imagine a student studying from a library book.

If the student has to visit the library every time they need information, it takes time and effort. Instead, they may keep important notes on their desk for quick access.

A cache works similarly.

Without Cache:
User → Application → Database → Response

With Cache:
User → Application → Cache → Response

If data exists in the cache (Cache Hit), it is returned immediately.
If data does not exist (Cache Miss), the application retrieves it from the database and stores it in the cache for future requests.

Common caching systems include Redis and Memcached. Large-scale applications use caching for user profiles, product information, search results, session management, and API responses.
questions.code_example:
User Request
     |
 Application
     |
    Cache
   /    \
Hit     Miss
 |        |
Data   Database
key_takeaways: Caching improves performance and reduces latency.
Frequently accessed data is stored temporarily.
Cache hits are faster than database queries.
Redis and Memcached are popular caching systems.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Database Replication
questions.question: What is Database Replication? Explain its working, types, advantages, and challenges.
questions.answer: Database Replication is the process of creating and maintaining multiple copies of the same database across different servers. Changes made to the primary database are synchronized with replica databases. Replication improves availability, fault tolerance, disaster recovery, and read scalability. It is widely used in distributed systems where millions of users access data simultaneously and a single database server cannot handle all requests efficiently.
questions.explanation:
Suppose an e-commerce website receives millions of product search requests every day.

If all requests are sent to a single database server, performance may degrade.

Replication solves this problem.

Primary Database:
Handles write operations.

Replica Databases:
Handle read operations.

User Requests
      |
Application
   /      \
Primary   Replica Servers

Advantages:
Improved availability.
Read scalability.
Disaster recovery.
Reduced database load.

Challenges:
Replication lag.
Consistency management.
Increased infrastructure complexity.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Database Sharding
questions.question: What is Database Sharding? Explain its architecture, benefits, challenges, and practical use cases.
questions.answer: Database Sharding is a horizontal scaling technique in which a large database is divided into smaller independent databases called shards. Each shard contains a subset of the overall data and operates as a separate database server. Sharding distributes storage and workload across multiple machines, allowing systems to handle massive amounts of data and traffic. It is commonly used by large-scale applications such as social media platforms, online gaming systems, and global e-commerce websites.
questions.explanation:
Imagine a library containing 100 million books.

Managing all books in a single room would be difficult.

Instead, books may be divided into different sections based on categories.

Similarly, databases can be divided into shards.

Shard 1:
Users 1 - 1 Million

Shard 2:
Users 1 Million - 2 Million

Shard 3:
Users 2 Million - 3 Million

Benefits:
Horizontal scalability.
Improved performance.
Reduced database bottlenecks.

Challenges:
Complex queries.
Cross-shard joins.
Data rebalancing.
Operational complexity.
questions.code_example:
Users
 |
Shard Router
 |--- Shard A
 |--- Shard B
 |--- Shard C
key_takeaways: Sharding divides databases into smaller parts.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: CAP Theorem
questions.question: What is the CAP Theorem? Explain Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance, and their trade-offs.
questions.answer: The CAP Theorem states that a distributed system can guarantee at most two of the following three properties simultaneously: Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance. Proposed by Eric Brewer, the theorem is one of the most fundamental concepts in distributed system design. It helps architects understand trade-offs when designing large-scale distributed systems.
questions.explanation:
Consistency:
Every node sees the same data at the same time.

Availability:
Every request receives a response.

Partition Tolerance:
The system continues operating despite network failures.

Example:
If network communication between servers fails, the system must choose between consistency and availability.

CP Systems:
Prioritize consistency.
Example: Traditional banking systems.

AP Systems:
Prioritize availability.
Example: Social media platforms.

Partition tolerance is mandatory in distributed environments because network failures are unavoidable.
questions.code_example:
CAP
 / | \
C  A  P
key_takeaways: CAP Theorem applies to distributed systems.
Only two properties can be fully guaranteed simultaneously.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Strong Consistency vs Eventual Consistency
questions.question: What are Strong Consistency and Eventual Consistency? Compare both approaches with examples.
questions.answer: Consistency models define how and when updates become visible across distributed systems. Strong Consistency guarantees that all users immediately see the latest data after an update. Eventual Consistency guarantees that all replicas will eventually become consistent, but temporary differences may exist. Choosing the appropriate consistency model depends on business requirements, performance expectations, and system architecture.
questions.explanation:
Strong Consistency Example:
Bank Account Balance.

If ₹1000 is deposited, every user must immediately see the updated balance.

Eventual Consistency Example:
Social Media Likes.

When a user likes a post, some users may see the updated count immediately while others may see it a few seconds later.

Strong consistency provides accuracy but may reduce performance.

Eventual consistency improves scalability and availability but allows temporary inconsistencies.
questions.code_example:
User A Update
      |
 Distributed System
      |
All Nodes Updated
key_takeaways: Banking systems often require strong consistency.
Social media commonly uses eventual consistency.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Content Delivery Network (CDN)
questions.question: What is a CDN? Explain its architecture, benefits, and importance in modern web applications.
questions.answer: A Content Delivery Network (CDN) is a geographically distributed network of servers that delivers static and dynamic content to users from locations closer to them. Instead of serving all requests from a single origin server, CDNs cache content at multiple edge locations worldwide. This reduces latency, improves page load speed, lowers bandwidth costs, and enhances availability.
questions.explanation:
Suppose a website is hosted in India.

A user in the United States requests an image.

Without CDN:
Request travels to India.

With CDN:
Image is served from a nearby US edge server.

Benefits:
Lower latency.
Faster content delivery.
Reduced server load.
Improved availability.
Protection against traffic spikes.

Popular CDNs include Cloudflare, Akamai, and Amazon CloudFront.
questions.code_example:
User
 |
Nearest CDN Edge
 |
Origin Server
key_takeaways: CDNs deliver content from nearby servers.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Message Queue
questions.question: What is a Message Queue? Explain its architecture, advantages, and common use cases.
questions.answer: A Message Queue is a communication mechanism that enables asynchronous interaction between different components of a distributed system. Producers send messages to a queue, and consumers process them independently. Message queues improve scalability, fault tolerance, reliability, and system decoupling. They are widely used in microservices, event-driven architectures, background job processing, and large-scale distributed applications.
questions.explanation:
Consider an e-commerce platform.

When a user places an order, multiple tasks must occur:

Send confirmation email.
Update inventory.
Generate invoice.
Notify warehouse.

Instead of performing everything immediately, the order service places messages into a queue.

Background workers process these tasks asynchronously.

This improves response time and prevents system overload.
questions.code_example:
Producer
   |
 Message Queue
   |
Consumer
key_takeaways: Common technologies include RabbitMQ and Kafka.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Microservices Architecture
questions.question: What is Microservices Architecture? Explain its advantages, disadvantages, and practical applications.
questions.answer: Microservices Architecture is a software design approach where an application is divided into small, independent services that communicate through APIs or messaging systems. Each service focuses on a specific business capability and can be developed, deployed, scaled, and maintained independently. Microservices have become popular because they improve scalability, flexibility, and development velocity in large organizations.
questions.explanation:
Consider an e-commerce platform.

Instead of one large application, it can be divided into:

User Service.
Product Service.
Order Service.
Payment Service.
Notification Service.

Advantages:
Independent deployment.
Technology flexibility.
Fault isolation.
Better scalability.

Disadvantages:
Operational complexity.
Network overhead.
Distributed debugging challenges.

Companies such as Netflix, Amazon, Uber, and Spotify use microservices extensively.
questions.code_example:
User Service
Product Service
Order Service
Payment Service
key_takeaways: Microservices divide applications into smaller services.
Each service has a specific responsibility.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: API Gateway
questions.question: What is an API Gateway? Explain its responsibilities and benefits in microservices architecture.
questions.answer: An API Gateway is a centralized entry point that sits between clients and backend services. It handles request routing, authentication, authorization, rate limiting, monitoring, logging, caching, and protocol translation. API Gateways simplify client interactions by providing a unified interface for accessing multiple services.
questions.explanation:
Without an API Gateway:

Clients communicate directly with multiple services.

With an API Gateway:

Clients communicate with one endpoint.

The gateway forwards requests to appropriate services.

Benefits:
Simplified client communication.
Improved security.
Centralized monitoring.
Reduced complexity.

API Gateways are commonly used in microservices ecosystems.
questions.code_example:
Client
  |
API Gateway
 |
Services
key_takeaways: API Gateway is a single entry point.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Rate Limiting
questions.question: What is Rate Limiting? Explain its purpose, techniques, and real-world applications.
questions.answer: Rate Limiting is a mechanism used to control the number of requests a client can make within a specific time period. It protects systems from abuse, prevents overload, improves fairness among users, and enhances security. Rate limiting is commonly implemented in APIs, web services, and cloud platforms.
questions.explanation:
Imagine a ticket booking website.

Without rate limiting, a malicious user could send thousands of requests per second, overwhelming the system.

Rate limiting restricts request frequency.

Common Algorithms:

Token Bucket.
Leaky Bucket.
Fixed Window Counter.
Sliding Window Counter.

Benefits:
Prevents abuse.
Protects infrastructure.
Improves service stability.
Ensures fair usage.
questions.code_example:
User Limit:
100 Requests / Minute
key_takeaways: Common in APIs and cloud services.
Uses algorithms such as Token Bucket.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: What is a Proxy Server?
questions.question: What is a Proxy Server in System Design? Explain its working, types, advantages, and practical applications.
questions.answer: A Proxy Server is an intermediary server that sits between clients and destination servers. Instead of clients communicating directly with backend services, requests are first sent to the proxy server, which then forwards them to the appropriate destination. Proxy servers improve security, performance, monitoring, access control, caching, and anonymity. They are widely used in enterprise networks, cloud platforms, content delivery systems, and large-scale distributed architectures. Proxy servers can also reduce server load by caching frequently requested content and filtering unwanted traffic.
questions.explanation:
Suppose a company has thousands of employees accessing the internet.

Instead of allowing direct internet access, all requests pass through a proxy server.

The proxy can:

Monitor requests.
Block malicious websites.
Cache frequently accessed pages.
Hide internal network details.

Types of Proxy Servers:

Forward Proxy:
Acts on behalf of clients.

Reverse Proxy:
Acts on behalf of servers.

Examples include Nginx, HAProxy, and Cloudflare.

Many modern applications use reverse proxies to improve security, scalability, and performance.
questions.code_example:
Client
  |
Proxy Server
  |
Destination Server
key_takeaways: Can hide client or server details.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Reverse Proxy
questions.question: What is a Reverse Proxy? Explain its architecture, advantages, and real-world usage.
questions.answer: A Reverse Proxy is a server positioned between clients and backend servers. Clients communicate with the reverse proxy, which forwards requests to backend servers and returns responses. Unlike forward proxies that represent clients, reverse proxies represent servers. Reverse proxies are commonly used for load balancing, SSL termination, caching, security, traffic routing, and high availability.
questions.explanation:
When millions of users access a website, exposing backend servers directly can create security and scalability challenges.

A reverse proxy provides a protective layer.

User Requests
      |
Reverse Proxy
  /    |    \
S1    S2    S3

Benefits:

Load balancing.
Improved security.
DDoS protection.
SSL certificate management.
Caching support.

Popular reverse proxy solutions include Nginx, HAProxy, Envoy, and Traefik.
questions.code_example:
Internet Users
      |
Reverse Proxy
      |
Backend Servers

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: What is Latency?
questions.question: What is Latency in System Design? Explain its causes, impact, and optimization techniques.
questions.answer: Latency is the time required for a request to travel from a client to a server and receive a response. It is usually measured in milliseconds (ms). Low latency is essential for providing responsive user experiences in applications such as online gaming, video conferencing, stock trading, and social media platforms. High latency can lead to slow performance, user frustration, and reduced engagement.
questions.explanation:
Suppose a user clicks a button in an application.

The request must travel through:

Network.
Load Balancer.
Application Server.
Database.

The response then returns to the user.

Factors affecting latency:

Network distance.
Database queries.
Server processing.
API calls.
Disk operations.

Optimization techniques:

Caching.
CDNs.
Database indexing.
Load balancing.
Query optimization.

Reducing latency is one of the primary goals of system architects.
questions.code_example:
User Request -> Server -> Response
Latency = Total Time Taken

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: What is Throughput?
questions.question: What is Throughput in System Design? Explain its significance and relationship with performance.
questions.answer: Throughput refers to the amount of work a system can process within a given time period. It is commonly measured in requests per second, transactions per second, messages per second, or data processed per second. Throughput is a critical performance metric because it indicates the capacity of a system to handle workloads efficiently.
questions.explanation:
Consider an API server.

If it can process 10,000 requests per second, its throughput is 10,000 RPS.

A system with high throughput can serve more users and handle larger workloads.

Factors affecting throughput include:

CPU resources.
Memory.
Network bandwidth.
Database performance.
Concurrency mechanisms.

System designers often optimize throughput when building large-scale platforms such as search engines, payment systems, and streaming services.
questions.code_example:
10000 Requests Processed
1 Second
Throughput = 10000 RPS
key_takeaways: Often measured in requests per second.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Horizontal Scaling vs Vertical Scaling
questions.question: Differentiate between Horizontal Scaling and Vertical Scaling. Explain advantages and disadvantages of both approaches.
questions.answer: Horizontal Scaling and Vertical Scaling are two approaches used to increase system capacity. Vertical Scaling involves increasing the resources of a single machine, such as CPU, RAM, or storage. Horizontal Scaling involves adding more machines to distribute the workload. Both approaches have advantages and trade-offs, and architects choose between them based on system requirements.
questions.explanation:
Vertical Scaling Example:

Upgrade server from:
8 GB RAM → 64 GB RAM.

Advantages:
Simple implementation.
No application changes.

Disadvantages:
Hardware limits.
Single point of failure.

Horizontal Scaling Example:

1 Server → 10 Servers → 100 Servers.

Advantages:
High availability.
Better fault tolerance.
Unlimited growth potential.

Disadvantages:
More complexity.
Distributed system challenges.

Large internet companies primarily use horizontal scaling.
questions.code_example:
Vertical: 1 Bigger Server
Horizontal: Multiple Servers

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Distributed System
questions.question: What is a Distributed System? Explain its characteristics, benefits, challenges, and applications.
questions.answer: A Distributed System is a collection of independent computers that work together as a single unified system. These computers communicate over a network and coordinate their actions to achieve common goals. Distributed systems provide scalability, fault tolerance, resource sharing, and high availability. Most modern internet applications are built using distributed system principles.
questions.explanation:
Examples include:

Google Search.
Netflix.
Amazon.
Facebook.
Uber.

Characteristics:

Multiple nodes.
Network communication.
Resource sharing.
Fault tolerance.
Scalability.

Challenges:

Network failures.
Consistency management.
Data synchronization.
Security.
Monitoring.

Distributed systems allow organizations to serve millions of users across different geographic regions.
questions.code_example:
Node1
Node2
Node3
Node4

Working Together

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Fault Tolerance
questions.question: What is Fault Tolerance in System Design? Explain techniques used to achieve fault tolerance.
questions.answer: Fault Tolerance is the ability of a system to continue functioning correctly even when some components fail. Since hardware failures, software bugs, network outages, and infrastructure issues are inevitable, modern systems are designed to tolerate failures without significant disruption. Fault tolerance improves reliability, availability, and business continuity.
questions.explanation:
Suppose a server crashes.

Without fault tolerance:
Application becomes unavailable.

With fault tolerance:
Traffic automatically shifts to healthy servers.

Techniques:

Redundancy.
Replication.
Failover systems.
Load balancing.
Health checks.
Automatic recovery.

Fault tolerance is essential for banking systems, cloud platforms, healthcare systems, and mission-critical applications.
questions.code_example:
Server A (Fails)
     |
Failover
     |
Server B

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Failover Mechanism
questions.question: What is Failover in System Design? Explain how it works and why it is important.
questions.answer: Failover is a mechanism that automatically transfers operations from a failed component to a backup component. The goal of failover is to minimize downtime and maintain service availability. Failover systems are commonly used in servers, databases, cloud platforms, networking equipment, and distributed applications.
questions.explanation:
Suppose a primary database server crashes.

Without failover:
Application stops working.

With failover:
Backup database automatically becomes active.

Types:

Active-Passive Failover.
Active-Active Failover.

Failover improves business continuity and reduces service interruptions during unexpected failures.
questions.code_example:
Primary Server
      |
 Failure
      |
Backup Server Activated

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Data Partitioning
questions.question: What is Data Partitioning? Explain its types, benefits, and challenges.
questions.answer: Data Partitioning is the process of dividing large datasets into smaller, manageable segments called partitions. Partitioning improves performance, scalability, and maintainability by distributing data across multiple storage units. It is commonly used in large databases and distributed systems handling massive volumes of information.
questions.explanation:
Types:

Horizontal Partitioning:
Rows are divided.

Vertical Partitioning:
Columns are divided.

Range Partitioning.
Hash Partitioning.
List Partitioning.

Benefits:
Improved performance.
Better scalability.
Reduced maintenance complexity.

Challenges:
Cross-partition queries.
Data balancing.
Operational complexity.
questions.code_example:
Users 1-1M -> Partition A
Users 1M-2M -> Partition B

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: High-Level Design (HLD)
questions.question: What is High-Level Design (HLD)? Explain its purpose and major components.
questions.answer: High-Level Design (HLD) is the architectural view of a system that describes major components, interactions, technologies, databases, APIs, and infrastructure. HLD focuses on the overall structure rather than implementation details. It helps stakeholders understand how the system will operate at a macro level.
questions.explanation:
When designing YouTube, HLD may include:

Load Balancers.
Application Servers.
Video Storage.
Databases.
CDN.
Recommendation Engine.

The goal is to understand system architecture before discussing implementation details.

HLD is commonly discussed during system design interviews.
questions.code_example:
Users
 |
Load Balancer
 |
Application Layer
 |
Database

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Low-Level Design (LLD)
questions.question: What is Low-Level Design (LLD)? Explain its purpose, components, and importance in software development.
questions.answer: Low-Level Design (LLD) is the detailed design phase of a software system where individual classes, objects, methods, interfaces, database schemas, APIs, and internal component interactions are defined. While High-Level Design focuses on the overall architecture, Low-Level Design focuses on implementation details. LLD provides developers with a clear blueprint for coding and ensures consistency across the development team. It helps identify design patterns, relationships between classes, object interactions, and module responsibilities before actual implementation begins.
questions.explanation:
Suppose we are designing a Food Delivery Application.

High-Level Design may define:
User Service
Restaurant Service
Order Service
Payment Service

Low-Level Design goes deeper and defines:
User Class
Order Class
Restaurant Class
Payment Interface
Database Tables
API Endpoints

LLD often includes UML diagrams, class diagrams, sequence diagrams, and database schema definitions. It helps developers understand exactly how the system should be implemented.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: SQL vs NoSQL Databases
questions.question: Compare SQL and NoSQL databases. Explain when to choose each type in system design.
questions.answer: SQL databases are relational databases that store data in structured tables with predefined schemas. They support ACID transactions and are ideal for applications requiring strong consistency. NoSQL databases are non-relational databases designed for scalability, flexibility, and handling large volumes of semi-structured or unstructured data. Choosing between SQL and NoSQL depends on business requirements, scalability needs, consistency requirements, and data structure complexity.
questions.explanation:
SQL Examples:
MySQL
PostgreSQL
Oracle
SQL Server

NoSQL Examples:
MongoDB
Cassandra
Redis
DynamoDB

Choose SQL When:
Banking systems.
Financial transactions.
Inventory management.

Choose NoSQL When:
Social media platforms.
Real-time analytics.
Large-scale distributed systems.

SQL prioritizes consistency.
NoSQL prioritizes scalability and flexibility.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Blob Storage
questions.question: What is Blob Storage? Explain its architecture, advantages, and common use cases.
questions.answer: Blob Storage (Binary Large Object Storage) is a storage solution designed to store large amounts of unstructured data such as images, videos, documents, backups, logs, and media files. Blob storage systems are highly scalable, durable, and cost-effective. They are commonly used in cloud platforms such as AWS S3, Azure Blob Storage, and Google Cloud Storage. Blob storage separates large files from application databases, improving overall system performance and scalability.
questions.explanation:
Imagine YouTube storing billions of videos.

Storing videos directly inside relational databases would be inefficient.

Instead:
Metadata → Database
Videos → Blob Storage

Benefits:
Massive scalability.
High durability.
Cost efficiency.
Global accessibility.

Blob storage is commonly used in content platforms, backup systems, document management systems, and cloud applications.
questions.code_example:
User Uploads Video
      |
Application
      |
Blob Storage

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Full-Text Search
questions.question: What is Full-Text Search? Explain how it works and why it is important in large-scale systems.
questions.answer: Full-Text Search is a search technique that allows users to search through large amounts of textual data efficiently. Unlike traditional database searches that perform exact matches, full-text search systems analyze text, tokenize words, build indexes, and support relevance ranking. Full-text search is widely used in search engines, e-commerce platforms, document management systems, and content-driven applications.
questions.explanation:
Suppose a user searches:

'Best wireless headphones'

A traditional database query may struggle with word variations.

A search engine such as Elasticsearch:

Tokenizes words.
Creates inverted indexes.
Ranks results by relevance.
Handles synonyms and stemming.

This allows users to receive fast and accurate search results even across millions of records.
questions.code_example:
User Query
      |
Search Engine
      |
Ranked Results

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Inverted Index
questions.question: What is an Inverted Index? Explain its structure and role in search engines.
questions.answer: An Inverted Index is a data structure used by search engines to quickly locate documents containing specific words. Instead of storing documents and searching through them one by one, an inverted index maps words to the documents in which they appear. This significantly improves search performance and scalability.
questions.explanation:
Suppose we have three documents:

Doc1: Java Programming
Doc2: Python Programming
Doc3: Java Database

Inverted Index:
Java -> Doc1, Doc3
Programming -> Doc1, Doc2
Python -> Doc2
Database -> Doc3

When a user searches for 'Java', the system immediately retrieves Doc1 and Doc3 without scanning every document.
questions.code_example:
Java -> [Doc1, Doc3]
Python -> [Doc2]

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Video Streaming Architecture
questions.question: How does a Video Streaming System like YouTube or Netflix work? Explain the architecture in detail.
questions.answer: A Video Streaming System is designed to store, process, distribute, and stream video content efficiently to millions of users. The architecture includes video upload services, transcoding pipelines, storage systems, CDNs, recommendation systems, metadata databases, and playback services. Such systems prioritize scalability, availability, low latency, and global content delivery.
questions.explanation:
Workflow:

User uploads video.

Video is stored in Blob Storage.

Transcoding service converts the video into multiple resolutions.

Metadata is stored in databases.

CDNs distribute videos globally.

Users stream content from nearby CDN servers.

Netflix and YouTube use thousands of servers worldwide to provide smooth playback even during massive traffic spikes.
questions.code_example: Upload -> Storage -> Transcoding -> CDN -> User
key_takeaways: Videos are stored separately from metadata.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Push Notification System
questions.question: How does a Push Notification System work? Explain its architecture and challenges.
questions.answer: A Push Notification System is designed to deliver real-time messages to users across mobile devices, web applications, and desktops. The system receives notification requests, processes them, stores delivery information, and sends messages through platforms such as Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) and Apple Push Notification Service (APNS). Push notification systems must handle scalability, reliability, delivery tracking, and user preferences.
questions.explanation:
Example:

User receives a message on WhatsApp.

Message Service generates notification.

Notification Service processes request.

FCM/APNS delivers notification.

Challenges:

Massive scale.
Duplicate notifications.
Delivery guarantees.
Rate limiting.
User preferences.

Modern systems often use queues and asynchronous processing for notification delivery.
questions.code_example:
Application
    |
Notification Service
    |
FCM / APNS
    |
User Device

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: System Monitoring
questions.question: What is System Monitoring? Explain its importance and major metrics tracked in distributed systems.
questions.answer: System Monitoring is the process of continuously observing the health, performance, availability, and behavior of software systems and infrastructure. Monitoring helps engineers detect issues early, troubleshoot failures, optimize performance, and ensure service reliability. Without monitoring, identifying production problems becomes extremely difficult.
questions.explanation:
Common Metrics:

CPU Usage.
Memory Usage.
Disk Utilization.
Network Traffic.
Request Latency.
Error Rates.
Throughput.
Availability.

Monitoring Tools:
Prometheus.
Grafana.
Datadog.
New Relic.
CloudWatch.

Monitoring provides visibility into system behavior and enables proactive issue resolution.
questions.code_example:
CPU: 70%
Memory: 65%
Latency: 120ms

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Centralized Logging
questions.question: What is Centralized Logging? Explain its architecture and benefits in distributed systems.
questions.answer: Centralized Logging is the practice of collecting logs from multiple servers, services, and applications into a centralized storage and analysis platform. Instead of manually checking logs on individual machines, engineers can search, analyze, and monitor logs from a single location. Centralized logging improves observability, debugging, auditing, and incident response.
questions.explanation:
Without Centralized Logging:

Logs are scattered across servers.

Troubleshooting becomes difficult.

With Centralized Logging:

All logs are aggregated.

Engineers can search events quickly.

Popular Tools:
ELK Stack.
Splunk.
Datadog.
Graylog.

Large organizations process billions of log events daily using centralized logging platforms.
questions.code_example:
Server1 Logs
Server2 Logs
Server3 Logs
      |
Central Log System

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Observability
questions.question: What is Observability in System Design? Explain its pillars and significance.
questions.answer: Observability is the ability to understand the internal state of a system by analyzing external outputs such as logs, metrics, and traces. Observability helps engineers diagnose issues, understand system behavior, and improve reliability. It is especially important in distributed systems where failures can occur across multiple services and infrastructure components.
questions.explanation:
Three Pillars of Observability:

Metrics:
Numerical measurements.

Logs:
Event records.

Traces:
Request journey across services.

Example:
If an API becomes slow, observability helps determine whether the issue originates from the application server, database, network, or external dependency.

Modern cloud-native systems rely heavily on observability practices.
questions.code_example: Metrics + Logs + Traces = Observability
key_takeaways: Built on metrics, logs, and traces.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Leader Election
questions.question: What is Leader Election in Distributed Systems? Explain why it is needed, how it works, and common algorithms used for leader election.
questions.answer: Leader Election is the process of selecting a single node from a group of distributed nodes to act as the coordinator or leader. The leader is responsible for making critical decisions, coordinating tasks, managing shared resources, and maintaining consistency across the system. Leader election is necessary because having multiple nodes perform the same coordination tasks simultaneously can lead to conflicts, inconsistencies, and duplicated work. Distributed systems use leader election to ensure organized operation, fault tolerance, and efficient coordination.
questions.explanation:
Imagine a classroom project with ten students.

If everyone tries to make decisions independently, confusion occurs.

Instead, one student is selected as the team leader.

Similarly, distributed systems elect a leader.

Responsibilities may include:

Coordinating tasks.
Managing resource allocation.
Handling distributed locks.
Maintaining cluster configuration.

If the leader fails, a new leader must be elected automatically.

Popular algorithms:

Bully Algorithm.
Ring Algorithm.
Raft.
ZooKeeper-based leader election.

Leader election is heavily used in distributed databases, Kubernetes clusters, and messaging systems.
questions.code_example:
Node1
Node2
Node3 -> Leader
Node4
Node5
key_takeaways: Leader election selects a coordinator node.
Used in Kubernetes and distributed databases.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Consensus Algorithms
questions.question: What are Consensus Algorithms? Explain their purpose and importance in distributed systems.
questions.answer: Consensus Algorithms are protocols used in distributed systems to ensure that multiple nodes agree on a single value or decision even when failures occur. Since distributed systems operate across different machines connected through networks, disagreements can arise due to network delays, crashes, or hardware failures. Consensus algorithms help maintain consistency, reliability, and correctness by ensuring all healthy nodes eventually reach the same decision.
questions.explanation:
Suppose five servers store banking transactions.

When a new transaction occurs, all servers must agree that the transaction is valid.

Without consensus, some servers may accept the transaction while others reject it.

Consensus algorithms solve this problem.

Popular Algorithms:

Raft.
Paxos.
Multi-Paxos.
Viewstamped Replication.

Applications:

Distributed databases.
Blockchain systems.
Cluster management.
Configuration management.

Consensus is one of the most important concepts in distributed system design.
questions.code_example:
Node1 -> Agree
Node2 -> Agree
Node3 -> Agree
Decision Accepted
key_takeaways: Raft and Paxos are popular algorithms.
Used in databases and distributed platforms.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Raft Consensus Algorithm
questions.question: What is the Raft Consensus Algorithm? Explain its working, components, and advantages.
questions.answer: Raft is a consensus algorithm designed to manage replicated logs in distributed systems. It was created to be easier to understand and implement compared to Paxos while providing similar guarantees. Raft ensures consistency among distributed nodes by electing a leader, replicating logs, and maintaining synchronized state across the cluster. It is widely used in modern distributed systems such as etcd, Consul, and Kubernetes control planes.
questions.explanation:
Raft consists of three roles:

Leader.
Follower.
Candidate.

Workflow:

Leader receives requests.
Leader replicates logs.
Followers acknowledge updates.
Data is committed after majority agreement.

If the leader fails:

Followers become candidates.
Voting occurs.
A new leader is elected.

Raft simplifies distributed consensus while maintaining reliability and consistency.
questions.code_example:
Leader
 |
Followers
 |
Replicated Logs

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Service Discovery
questions.question: What is Service Discovery in Microservices Architecture? Explain its importance and implementation approaches.
questions.answer: Service Discovery is the mechanism through which services dynamically locate and communicate with other services in a distributed environment. Since microservices can scale up, scale down, restart, or move across servers, their network locations may change frequently. Service discovery ensures that services can automatically find each other without requiring hardcoded addresses.
questions.explanation:
Suppose an Order Service needs to communicate with a Payment Service.

Instead of storing a fixed server address, it asks a service registry.

The registry provides the latest location of the Payment Service.

Approaches:

Client-Side Discovery.
Server-Side Discovery.

Popular Tools:

Consul.
Eureka.
ZooKeeper.
Kubernetes Service Discovery.

Service discovery is essential in cloud-native and microservices architectures.
questions.code_example:
Order Service
      |
Service Registry
      |
Payment Service

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Circuit Breaker Pattern
questions.question: What is the Circuit Breaker Pattern? Explain its working and benefits in distributed systems.
questions.answer: The Circuit Breaker Pattern is a fault tolerance design pattern that prevents repeated calls to failing services. Instead of continuously sending requests to an unhealthy service, the circuit breaker temporarily blocks requests and allows the system to recover. This prevents cascading failures and improves overall system stability.
questions.explanation:
Imagine an Order Service calling a Payment Service.

If the Payment Service becomes unavailable:

Without Circuit Breaker:
Thousands of requests continue failing.

With Circuit Breaker:
Requests are stopped temporarily.
Fallback responses are returned.

States:

Closed.
Open.
Half-Open.

Benefits:
Reduced failures.
Improved stability.
Faster recovery.
Better user experience.
questions.code_example:
Request
  |
Circuit Breaker
  |
Payment Service
key_takeaways: Uses Closed, Open, and Half-Open states.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Saga Pattern
questions.question: What is the Saga Pattern? Explain how distributed transactions are managed using Saga Architecture.
questions.answer: The Saga Pattern is a design pattern used to manage distributed transactions across multiple microservices. Instead of using a single global transaction, a saga breaks a business process into multiple local transactions. If a step fails, compensating transactions are executed to undo previously completed operations. This approach improves scalability and avoids the limitations of traditional distributed transactions.
questions.explanation:
Example: Online Order Placement.

Order Service creates order.
Payment Service processes payment.
Inventory Service reserves stock.
Shipping Service schedules delivery.

If inventory reservation fails:

Payment is refunded.
Order is cancelled.

These rollback operations are called compensating transactions.

Saga patterns are widely used in modern microservices systems.
questions.code_example: Order -> Payment -> Inventory -> Shipping

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Read Replicas
questions.question: What are Read Replicas? Explain their architecture, advantages, and limitations.
questions.answer: Read Replicas are copies of a primary database that handle read-only workloads. They help distribute query traffic, improve scalability, and reduce load on the primary database. Read replicas are commonly used in applications where read operations significantly outnumber write operations.
questions.explanation:
Suppose an e-commerce website receives:

1 million product searches.
10,000 product updates.

Instead of sending all requests to one database:

Writes → Primary Database.
Reads → Read Replicas.

Benefits:
Improved performance.
Better scalability.
Reduced primary load.

Limitations:
Replication lag.
Eventual consistency issues.

Large-scale applications frequently use multiple replicas.
questions.code_example:
Primary DB
 |
Replica1
Replica2
Replica3

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Cache Invalidation
questions.question: What is Cache Invalidation? Why is it considered one of the hardest problems in system design?
questions.answer: Cache Invalidation is the process of removing or updating stale data stored in a cache when the underlying source data changes. It ensures users receive accurate and up-to-date information. Cache invalidation is difficult because systems must balance performance benefits with data consistency requirements. Incorrect invalidation strategies can result in stale data, inconsistent user experiences, and application bugs.
questions.explanation:
Suppose a product price changes from ₹500 to ₹400.

Database contains ₹400.
Cache still contains ₹500.

Users may see incorrect pricing.

Common Strategies:

Time-To-Live (TTL).
Write-Through Cache.
Write-Behind Cache.
Cache Eviction.
Explicit Invalidation.

Large-scale systems invest significant effort into designing efficient cache invalidation mechanisms.
questions.code_example:
Database Updated
      |
Invalidate Cache
      |
New Data Loaded
key_takeaways: One of the hardest distributed system problems.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Cache Eviction Policies
questions.question: What are Cache Eviction Policies? Explain common eviction algorithms and their use cases.
questions.answer: Cache Eviction Policies determine which items should be removed from a cache when storage capacity becomes full. Since cache memory is limited, systems must intelligently decide which entries to keep and which to remove. Effective eviction policies maximize cache efficiency and improve overall performance.
questions.explanation:
Popular Policies:

LRU (Least Recently Used)
Removes least recently accessed items.

LFU (Least Frequently Used)
Removes least frequently accessed items.

FIFO (First In First Out)
Removes oldest entries.

Random Replacement
Removes random entries.

Example:
If a cache can store only 100 items and item 101 arrives, one existing item must be removed according to the selected policy.
questions.code_example:
Cache Full
 |
Apply LRU Policy
 |
Remove Old Entry

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Authentication vs Authorization
questions.question: Differentiate between Authentication and Authorization in System Design with practical examples.
questions.answer: Authentication is the process of verifying the identity of a user, service, or system. Authorization is the process of determining what actions an authenticated entity is allowed to perform. Authentication answers the question 'Who are you?' while authorization answers the question 'What are you allowed to do?'. Both are fundamental security concepts used in modern software systems.
questions.explanation:
Example:

A user logs into an application using email and password.

Authentication:
Verifies the user's identity.

Authorization:
Determines whether the user can access admin features, edit records, or view reports.

Common Authentication Methods:

Passwords.
OAuth.
JWT.
Multi-Factor Authentication.

Authorization Models:

Role-Based Access Control (RBAC).
Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC).
questions.code_example:
Login -> Authentication
Access Resource -> Authorization

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: JWT Authentication
questions.question: What is JWT (JSON Web Token)? Explain its structure, working, advantages, disadvantages, and use cases in modern system design.
questions.answer: JWT (JSON Web Token) is a compact, self-contained, and secure mechanism used for transmitting information between parties as a JSON object. It is commonly used for authentication and authorization in web applications, mobile applications, APIs, and microservices architectures. JWT contains encoded user information and is digitally signed to prevent tampering. Because JWT is stateless, servers do not need to store session information, making it highly scalable for distributed systems. Modern applications frequently use JWT for Single Sign-On (SSO), API authentication, and user session management.
questions.explanation:
A JWT consists of three parts:

Header
Contains token type and algorithm.

Payload
Contains user information and claims.

Signature
Verifies token authenticity.

Workflow:

User logs in.
Server verifies credentials.
Server generates JWT.
Client stores JWT.
Client sends JWT with future requests.
Server validates JWT.

Advantages:
Stateless authentication.
Scalability.
Cross-platform support.

Disadvantages:
Token revocation complexity.
Larger payload sizes.
Security risks if tokens are exposed.
questions.code_example: Header.Payload.Signature
key_takeaways: JWT is widely used for authentication.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: OAuth 2.0
questions.question: What is OAuth 2.0? Explain its architecture, flow, advantages, and real-world applications.
questions.answer: OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework that allows third-party applications to access resources on behalf of a user without exposing the user's credentials. It separates authentication from authorization and provides secure delegated access. OAuth is widely used by platforms such as Google, Facebook, GitHub, LinkedIn, and Microsoft to allow users to log into external applications securely.
questions.explanation:
Example:

A user wants to log into a website using Google.

Instead of sharing the Google password with the website:

User is redirected to Google.
Google authenticates the user.
Google provides an access token.
The application uses the token to access approved information.

Benefits:
Improved security.
Reduced password sharing.
Delegated access.
Better user experience.

OAuth is one of the most commonly used authorization standards on the internet.
questions.code_example:
User -> Google Login
      |
 Access Token
      |
Application

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Rate Limiting Algorithms
questions.question: Explain the major Rate Limiting Algorithms used in system design and compare their advantages.
questions.answer: Rate Limiting Algorithms are mechanisms used to restrict the number of requests clients can make within a specified period. They help prevent abuse, protect infrastructure, and ensure fair resource usage. Different algorithms provide different trade-offs between accuracy, complexity, and performance. Choosing the correct algorithm depends on system requirements and traffic patterns.
questions.explanation:
Common Algorithms:

Fixed Window Counter:
Simple implementation but may allow traffic spikes.

Sliding Window Counter:
More accurate request control.

Token Bucket:
Allows controlled bursts.

Leaky Bucket:
Provides smooth traffic flow.

Example:
API allows 100 requests per minute.

These algorithms ensure no user can overwhelm the system while maintaining good user experience.
questions.code_example:
Limit: 100 Requests/Minute
User -> Rate Limiter -> API

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Object Storage vs Block Storage
questions.question: Compare Object Storage and Block Storage. Explain their architectures, advantages, disadvantages, and use cases.
questions.answer: Object Storage and Block Storage are two popular storage models used in modern system architectures. Object Storage stores data as objects containing data, metadata, and unique identifiers. Block Storage divides data into fixed-size blocks and stores them separately. Both approaches serve different purposes depending on application requirements, scalability needs, and performance expectations.
questions.explanation:
Object Storage:

Stores files as objects.
Highly scalable.
Suitable for images, videos, backups.
Examples: AWS S3, Azure Blob Storage.

Block Storage:

Stores data in blocks.
High performance.
Suitable for databases and operating systems.
Examples: AWS EBS.

Object Storage prioritizes scalability.
Block Storage prioritizes performance and low latency.
questions.code_example:
Object Storage -> Images, Videos
Block Storage -> Databases

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Data Compression
questions.question: What is Data Compression? Explain its role, techniques, advantages, and trade-offs in large-scale systems.
questions.answer: Data Compression is the process of reducing the size of data to save storage space and reduce network bandwidth consumption. Compression improves storage efficiency, reduces costs, accelerates data transfer, and enhances scalability. Large-scale systems frequently use compression for logs, backups, databases, images, videos, and network communication.
questions.explanation:
Suppose a 100 MB file is compressed to 20 MB.

Benefits:
Faster transfer.
Reduced storage costs.
Lower bandwidth usage.

Types:

Lossless Compression:
Original data can be fully restored.
Examples: ZIP, GZIP.

Lossy Compression:
Some information is discarded.
Examples: JPEG, MP3.

Choosing the correct compression strategy depends on the application.
questions.code_example:
100 MB File
   |
Compression
   |
20 MB File

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Event-Driven Architecture
questions.question: What is Event-Driven Architecture (EDA)? Explain its components, benefits, and challenges.
questions.answer: Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) is a software architecture pattern where components communicate through events. Instead of directly invoking services, systems publish events whenever significant actions occur. Other services subscribe to relevant events and react accordingly. This approach improves scalability, flexibility, and decoupling in distributed systems.
questions.explanation:
Example:

User places an order.

Order Service publishes an event.

Payment Service processes payment.
Inventory Service updates stock.
Notification Service sends confirmation.

Benefits:
Loose coupling.
Scalability.
Flexibility.
Asynchronous processing.

Challenges:
Debugging complexity.
Event ordering.
Consistency management.
questions.code_example:
Order Created Event
      |
Event Bus
 /    |    \
Payment Inventory Notification

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Apache Kafka
questions.question: What is Apache Kafka? Explain its architecture, components, and use cases in distributed systems.
questions.answer: Apache Kafka is a distributed event streaming platform designed for high-throughput, fault-tolerant, and scalable real-time data processing. Kafka enables applications to publish, subscribe, store, and process streams of records efficiently. It is widely used for event streaming, log aggregation, real-time analytics, monitoring, and microservices communication.
questions.explanation:
Core Components:

Producer:
Publishes messages.

Broker:
Stores messages.

Topic:
Logical category of messages.

Consumer:
Reads messages.

Kafka Cluster:
Multiple brokers working together.

Companies use Kafka for:
Real-time analytics.
Fraud detection.
Activity tracking.
Log processing.
Event-driven systems.
questions.code_example: Producer -> Kafka Topic -> Consumer
key_takeaways: Kafka is a distributed streaming platform.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Batch Processing vs Stream Processing
questions.question: Differentiate between Batch Processing and Stream Processing with examples and use cases.
questions.answer: Batch Processing and Stream Processing are two approaches used to process data. Batch Processing handles large volumes of accumulated data at scheduled intervals, while Stream Processing processes data continuously as it arrives. Both approaches serve different business requirements and performance objectives.
questions.explanation:
Batch Processing Example:

Daily payroll processing.
Monthly reports.
Data warehouse updates.

Stream Processing Example:

Fraud detection.
Stock trading.
Live analytics.
Social media feeds.

Batch processing is efficient for large periodic jobs.

Stream processing is ideal for real-time decision-making and event handling.
questions.code_example:
Batch -> Daily Reports
Stream -> Real-Time Events

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Hotspot Problem
questions.question: What is the Hotspot Problem in Distributed Systems? Explain its causes, impact, and solutions.
questions.answer: The Hotspot Problem occurs when a disproportionate amount of traffic, requests, or data is directed toward a specific server, partition, database shard, or resource. As a result, that resource becomes overloaded while others remain underutilized. Hotspots can significantly impact performance, scalability, and availability in distributed systems.
questions.explanation:
Example:

Suppose user IDs are assigned sequentially.

Most new users are stored in the latest shard.

That shard receives most writes.

Consequences:
Increased latency.
Reduced throughput.
System instability.

Solutions:
Hash-based partitioning.
Load balancing.
Consistent hashing.
Traffic distribution.
Data rebalancing.
questions.code_example:
Shard A -> 90% Traffic
Shard B -> 5%
Shard C -> 5%

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Consistent Hashing
questions.question: What is Consistent Hashing? Explain how it works and why it is important in scalable systems.
questions.answer: Consistent Hashing is a partitioning technique used to distribute data across multiple servers while minimizing data movement when servers are added or removed. Traditional hashing often requires massive redistribution of data whenever infrastructure changes. Consistent hashing solves this problem by organizing nodes and keys on a logical hash ring, allowing only a small portion of data to be reassigned during topology changes.
questions.explanation:
Without Consistent Hashing:

Adding one server may require redistributing nearly all data.

With Consistent Hashing:

Only a small subset of keys moves.

Benefits:
Improved scalability.
Efficient server additions.
Efficient server removals.
Better load distribution.
Reduced operational overhead.

Used by systems such as DynamoDB, Cassandra, Redis clusters, and distributed caches.
questions.code_example:
Hash Ring
[Node A] -> [Node B] -> [Node C]

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Load Balancing Algorithms
questions.question: What are Load Balancing Algorithms? Explain the most commonly used algorithms, their advantages, disadvantages, and practical use cases.
questions.answer: Load Balancing Algorithms are strategies used by load balancers to determine how incoming requests should be distributed among backend servers. The primary goal is to ensure efficient resource utilization, prevent server overload, improve availability, and maintain low response times. Different algorithms are suitable for different workloads depending on server capacity, traffic patterns, and system architecture. Modern distributed systems rely heavily on load balancing algorithms to support scalability and fault tolerance.
questions.explanation:
Common Algorithms:

Round Robin:
Requests are distributed sequentially among servers.

Weighted Round Robin:
More powerful servers receive more requests.

Least Connections:
Requests go to the server with the fewest active connections.

IP Hash:
Requests from the same client are routed consistently to the same server.

Least Response Time:
Traffic is directed to the fastest server.

Example:
If three servers exist and one server is twice as powerful as the others, Weighted Round Robin ensures it handles more requests. Choosing the right algorithm significantly impacts application performance and resource utilization.
questions.code_example:
Request1 -> Server1
Request2 -> Server2
Request3 -> Server3
key_takeaways: Round Robin is simple and popular.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Split Brain Problem
questions.question: What is the Split Brain Problem in Distributed Systems? Explain its causes, consequences, and prevention techniques.
questions.answer: The Split Brain Problem occurs when a distributed cluster becomes partitioned and multiple nodes incorrectly believe they are the primary leader. As a result, conflicting operations may occur simultaneously, causing data inconsistency, corruption, and system instability. Split brain situations are particularly dangerous in distributed databases, storage systems, and cluster management platforms where consistency is critical.
questions.explanation:
Imagine a cluster with two servers.

Server A and Server B communicate normally.

A network failure occurs.

Server A believes Server B has failed.
Server B believes Server A has failed.

Both become leaders.

Now conflicting writes occur.

Solutions include:

Quorum-based voting.
Consensus algorithms such as Raft.
Witness nodes.
Cluster fencing.

Preventing split brain is essential for maintaining consistency and reliability in distributed environments.
questions.code_example:
Network Failure
   |
Node A -> Leader
Node B -> Leader

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Quorum-Based Systems
questions.question: What is Quorum in Distributed Databases? Explain read quorum, write quorum, and their significance.
questions.answer: A Quorum is the minimum number of nodes that must participate in a distributed operation before it is considered successful. Quorum mechanisms help maintain consistency while balancing availability and fault tolerance. Distributed databases use read and write quorums to ensure that data remains synchronized across replicas even when failures occur.
questions.explanation:
Suppose a database has 5 replicas.

Write Quorum (W):
Minimum replicas required to accept a write.

Read Quorum (R):
Minimum replicas required for a read.

If:
N = 5
W = 3
R = 3

Then R + W > N.

This guarantees overlap between reads and writes, improving consistency.

Quorum systems are used in Cassandra, DynamoDB, Riak, and other distributed databases.
questions.code_example:
Total Nodes = 5
Write Quorum = 3
Read Quorum = 3

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Database Indexing
questions.question: What is Database Indexing? Explain its working, advantages, disadvantages, and practical applications.
questions.answer: Database Indexing is a technique used to improve data retrieval performance by creating additional data structures that allow faster searching. Instead of scanning every row in a table, the database can use an index to locate records efficiently. Indexing significantly improves query performance, especially for large datasets containing millions of records.
questions.explanation:
Imagine a book containing 1,000 pages.

Without an index, finding a topic requires scanning page by page.

With an index, you can immediately locate the desired page.

Similarly, database indexes provide shortcuts for data retrieval.

Advantages:
Faster queries.
Reduced search time.
Improved application performance.

Disadvantages:
Additional storage usage.
Slower write operations.
Maintenance overhead.

Indexes are fundamental to modern database optimization.
questions.code_example:
CREATE INDEX idx_user_email
ON Users(email);

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Cache Aside Pattern
questions.question: What is the Cache Aside Pattern? Explain its workflow, advantages, disadvantages, and use cases.
questions.answer: The Cache Aside Pattern is one of the most commonly used caching strategies. In this pattern, the application first checks the cache for data. If the data is found, it is returned immediately. If the data is missing, the application retrieves it from the database, stores it in the cache, and then returns it to the user. This approach allows applications to control cache population and maintain flexibility.
questions.explanation:
Workflow:

Application checks cache.

Cache Hit:
Return data.

Cache Miss:
Query database.
Store result in cache.
Return data.

Advantages:
Simple implementation.
Reduced database load.
Improved performance.

Challenges:
Cache invalidation.
Cold starts.
Potential stale data.

Cache Aside is widely used in Redis-based architectures.
questions.code_example:
Check Cache
   |
Hit -> Return Data
Miss -> Database -> Cache

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Write Through Cache
questions.question: What is Write Through Caching? Explain how it works and compare it with Cache Aside.
questions.answer: Write Through Cache is a caching strategy in which every write operation updates both the cache and the database simultaneously. This ensures that cached data remains consistent with the source of truth. Unlike Cache Aside, where the application manages cache population during reads, Write Through automatically keeps cache contents synchronized after writes.
questions.explanation:
Workflow:

Application writes data.

Cache updated.
Database updated.
Operation succeeds.

Advantages:
Strong consistency.
Reduced cache misses.
Simplified reads.

Disadvantages:
Higher write latency.
Additional storage overhead.

Write Through is useful when read performance is critical and data consistency must be maintained.
questions.code_example:
Application
   |
Cache Update
   |
Database Update
key_takeaways: Write Through updates cache and database together.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Write Back Cache
questions.question: What is Write Back Caching? Explain its architecture, benefits, risks, and use cases.
questions.answer: Write Back Caching is a caching strategy where write operations are initially stored in the cache and written to the database later, often asynchronously. This approach improves write performance because applications do not wait for database updates to complete. However, it introduces risks related to data durability and consistency if failures occur before data is persisted.
questions.explanation:
Workflow:

Application writes data.

Cache updated immediately.

Database updated later.

Advantages:
Very fast writes.
Reduced database pressure.
Improved throughput.

Disadvantages:
Potential data loss.
Recovery complexity.
Consistency challenges.

Write Back caching is often used in high-performance systems where speed is prioritized.
questions.code_example:
Application
   |
Cache
   |
Async Database Write

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: REST API Design Principles
questions.question: What are REST APIs? Explain REST principles, advantages, and best practices.
questions.answer: REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style used for designing web services and APIs. REST APIs use standard HTTP methods and resources to enable communication between clients and servers. REST emphasizes simplicity, scalability, stateless communication, and resource-oriented design. It has become one of the most widely adopted API styles in modern software development.
questions.explanation:
HTTP Methods:

GET -> Retrieve Data.
POST -> Create Data.
PUT -> Update Data.
DELETE -> Remove Data.

Example:

GET /users
POST /orders
PUT /products/10
DELETE /comments/5

REST Best Practices:

Use meaningful URLs.
Use proper HTTP methods.
Maintain statelessness.
Version APIs.
Return appropriate status codes.
questions.code_example: GET /api/users/101

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: GraphQL vs REST
questions.question: Compare GraphQL and REST APIs. Explain advantages, disadvantages, and when to use each.
questions.answer: GraphQL and REST are two popular approaches for building APIs. REST exposes multiple endpoints representing resources, while GraphQL exposes a single endpoint through which clients can request exactly the data they need. GraphQL reduces over-fetching and under-fetching problems but introduces additional complexity compared to REST.
questions.explanation:
REST Example:

/users
/orders
/products

GraphQL Example:

/graphql

Advantages of GraphQL:
Flexible queries.
Reduced network calls.
Efficient data retrieval.

Advantages of REST:
Simplicity.
Mature ecosystem.
Easy caching.

GraphQL is useful for complex frontends while REST remains popular for general-purpose APIs.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Distributed Locking
questions.question: What is Distributed Locking? Explain its purpose, challenges, and implementation approaches.
questions.answer: Distributed Locking is a mechanism used to ensure that only one process, service, or node can access a shared resource at a given time in a distributed environment. Since multiple servers may attempt to modify the same resource concurrently, distributed locks help prevent race conditions, duplicate operations, and data inconsistencies. Distributed locking is widely used in microservices, distributed databases, task schedulers, and financial systems.
questions.explanation:
Suppose multiple servers process payment requests.

Without locking:

Two servers may process the same transaction.

With distributed locking:

One server acquires the lock.
Other servers wait.

After completion:
Lock is released.

Implementation Approaches:

Redis Locks.
ZooKeeper.
etcd.
Database Locks.

Distributed locking is essential when strong coordination is required.
questions.code_example:
Acquire Lock
   |
Perform Task
   |
Release Lock
key_takeaways: Can be implemented using Redis or ZooKeeper.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Designing a URL Shortener
questions.question: How would you design a URL Shortener service like Bitly or TinyURL? Explain the architecture, components, database design, scalability considerations, challenges, and optimizations in detail.
questions.answer: A URL Shortener is a service that converts long URLs into short, unique URLs that are easier to share, store, and manage. The system accepts a long URL from a user, generates a unique short code, stores the mapping, and redirects users to the original URL whenever the shortened link is accessed. Designing such a system involves handling URL generation, redirection performance, scalability, analytics tracking, reliability, caching, database design, and security considerations. Since popular URL shorteners may process billions of redirects daily, the system must be highly available, fault tolerant, and capable of serving requests with extremely low latency.
questions.explanation:
Functional Requirements:

Create short URLs.
Redirect short URLs.
Track click analytics.
Support custom aliases.
Support URL expiration.

Non-Functional Requirements:

High availability.
Low latency.
Massive scalability.
Fault tolerance.

Architecture:

User -> API Server -> URL Service -> Database
                        |
                      Cache

Key Challenges:

Generating unique IDs.
Preventing collisions.
Handling billions of redirects.
Managing analytics.

Optimization Techniques:

Caching popular URLs.
Database partitioning.
CDN usage.
Read replicas.

Popular ID generation techniques include Base62 encoding, Snowflake IDs, and distributed sequence generators.
questions.code_example:
https://tinyurl.com/AbX92
      |
Database Mapping
      |
https://very-long-url-example.com
key_takeaways: URL shorteners map short codes to long URLs.
Unique ID generation is a major challenge.
Scalability is essential for large deployments.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Designing a Chat Application
questions.question: How would you design a real-time chat application like WhatsApp, Telegram, or Messenger? Explain the architecture, scalability requirements, message delivery mechanisms, and reliability considerations.
questions.answer: A real-time chat application enables users to exchange messages instantly across devices and platforms.
