Designing such a system requires support for message delivery, online status tracking, message persistence, media sharing, group conversations, synchronization across devices, and reliable notifications. Since chat platforms may handle billions of messages per day, the architecture must support low latency communication, horizontal scalability, high availability, and fault tolerance. Modern chat systems typically combine WebSockets, message queues, distributed databases, and push notification services.
questions.explanation:
Core Components:

Authentication Service.
User Service.
Message Service.
Notification Service.
Media Service.
Presence Service.

Workflow:

User sends message.
Message stored in database.
Message pushed to recipient.
Delivery status updated.
Read receipts tracked.

Challenges:

Offline users.
Message ordering.
Global scale.
Group messaging.
Media storage.

Technologies:

WebSockets.
Kafka.
Redis.
Distributed Databases.
Push Notifications.

Large chat systems must guarantee message durability while maintaining near real-time delivery.
questions.code_example:
Sender
  |
Chat Server
  |
Recipient

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Designing a News Feed System
questions.question: How would you design a News Feed system like Facebook, Instagram, or LinkedIn? Explain feed generation strategies, scalability challenges, and architecture decisions.
questions.answer: A News Feed System generates personalized content streams for users based on their connections, interests, and activities. The system must efficiently handle content creation, feed generation, ranking, personalization, caching, and content delivery. Since social media platforms serve hundreds of millions of users, feed systems must process enormous amounts of data while maintaining low latency and high availability.
questions.explanation:
Feed Generation Approaches:

Fan-Out on Write:
Feed updates generated when content is created.

Fan-Out on Read:
Feed generated when users request it.

Architecture:

User Service.
Post Service.
Feed Generator.
Ranking Engine.
Cache Layer.
Database.

Challenges:

Celebrity users with millions of followers.
Feed ranking.
Data freshness.
Storage costs.
Scalability.

Hybrid approaches are often used to balance performance and storage requirements.
questions.code_example:
Post Created
      |
Feed Generator
      |
User Feeds
key_takeaways: Social networks rely heavily on feed optimization.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Designing a Distributed Cache
questions.question: How would you design a Distributed Cache system like Redis Cluster? Explain architecture, partitioning, replication, and fault tolerance mechanisms.
questions.answer: A Distributed Cache is a caching system that spans multiple servers and provides fast access to frequently used data. It is designed to reduce database load, improve response times, and support large-scale applications. Distributed caches partition data across nodes and often implement replication to ensure availability and durability. Such systems must handle node failures, data distribution, consistency, and scalability efficiently.
questions.explanation:
Core Features:

High-speed data access.
Horizontal scaling.
Replication.
Automatic failover.
Data partitioning.

Challenges:

Cache invalidation.
Data consistency.
Node failures.
Rebalancing.

Technologies:

Redis Cluster.
Memcached.
Hazelcast.

Distributed caches are often positioned between application servers and databases to reduce expensive database operations.
questions.code_example:
App
 |
Distributed Cache
 |
Database

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Designing a File Storage Service
questions.question: How would you design a cloud file storage service similar to Google Drive or Dropbox?
questions.answer: A cloud file storage service allows users to upload, store, synchronize, share, and retrieve files across devices. The system must support massive storage requirements, file versioning, synchronization, sharing permissions, security, backups, and disaster recovery. Scalability and durability are critical because users trust the platform with important personal and business files.
questions.explanation:
Components:

Authentication Service.
File Metadata Service.
Blob Storage.
Search Service.
Sharing Service.
Notification Service.

Features:

File Uploads.
File Downloads.
Version Control.
Sharing Permissions.
Device Synchronization.

Challenges:

Storage optimization.
Data durability.
Global distribution.
Large file uploads.
Conflict resolution.

Files are typically stored in object storage while metadata is maintained in databases.
questions.code_example: User -> Upload Service -> Blob Storage

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Designing a Search Engine
questions.question: How would you design a search engine like Google? Explain crawling, indexing, ranking, and query processing components.
questions.answer: A Search Engine is a large-scale information retrieval system designed to discover, index, rank, and retrieve relevant documents from massive datasets. Search engines continuously crawl web pages, process content, build indexes, and rank results according to relevance. Designing such a system requires handling enormous data volumes, real-time updates, low latency queries, and sophisticated ranking algorithms.
questions.explanation:
Major Components:

Web Crawler.
Indexer.
Inverted Index.
Ranking Engine.
Query Processor.
Storage Layer.

Workflow:

Crawl pages.
Extract content.
Build indexes.
Process queries.
Rank results.
Return responses.

Challenges:

Massive scale.
Freshness of content.
Spam detection.
Ranking quality.
Distributed storage.

Search engines are among the most complex distributed systems ever built.
questions.code_example: Crawler -> Indexer -> Search Engine -> Results

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Designing a Recommendation System
questions.question: How would you design a recommendation system like Netflix, YouTube, or Amazon?
questions.answer: A Recommendation System suggests relevant content, products, videos, or services to users based on behavior, preferences, and historical interactions. The goal is to increase engagement, improve user experience, and drive business growth. Recommendation systems process massive amounts of user activity data and use machine learning models to predict what users are most likely to consume next.
questions.explanation:
Inputs:

Watch History.
Search History.
Purchase History.
User Preferences.
Ratings.

Approaches:

Collaborative Filtering.
Content-Based Filtering.
Hybrid Models.

Challenges:

Cold Start Problem.
Data Sparsity.
Real-Time Updates.
Scalability.

Recommendation engines are among the most valuable components in modern digital platforms.
questions.code_example: User Activity -> Recommendation Engine -> Suggestions
key_takeaways: Machine learning plays a major role.
Cold start is a common challenge.

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Designing a Web Crawler
questions.question: How would you design a large-scale Web Crawler capable of crawling billions of web pages?
questions.answer: A Web Crawler is a system that automatically discovers and downloads web pages for indexing and analysis. Large-scale crawlers must operate efficiently across billions of pages while respecting website policies, avoiding duplicate content, and maintaining freshness. The architecture must support distributed processing, scheduling, URL management, storage, and fault tolerance.
questions.explanation:
Components:

URL Frontier.
Scheduler.
Crawler Workers.
Parser.
Storage Layer.
Deduplication System.

Challenges:

Massive scale.
Duplicate pages.
Rate limiting.
Politeness policies.
Freshness updates.

Web crawlers form the foundation of search engines and many internet-scale data platforms.
questions.code_example: URL Queue -> Crawler -> Parser -> Storage

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: Designing a Real-Time Analytics Platform
questions.question: How would you design a real-time analytics platform capable of processing millions of events per second?
questions.answer: A Real-Time Analytics Platform collects, processes, aggregates, and visualizes events as they occur. Such systems are used for monitoring, business intelligence, fraud detection, operational dashboards, and user behavior analysis. The architecture must support high-throughput ingestion, low-latency processing, fault tolerance, and scalable storage.
questions.explanation:
Workflow:

Events Generated.
Events Ingested.
Stream Processing.
Aggregation.
Visualization.

Components:

Kafka.
Stream Processors.
Data Warehouse.
Dashboards.
Alerting Systems.

Challenges:

High throughput.
Data consistency.
Latency requirements.
Storage optimization.

Real-time analytics powers many modern business intelligence systems.
questions.code_example: Events -> Kafka -> Processing -> Dashboard

questions.subject: System Design
questions.title: How to Approach Any System Design Interview
questions.question: What is the proper framework for solving System Design Interview questions? Explain a structured step-by-step approach used by experienced engineers.
questions.answer: A System Design Interview evaluates a candidate's ability to design scalable, reliable, maintainable, and efficient software systems. Rather than focusing solely on the final architecture, interviewers assess how candidates analyze requirements, make trade-offs, identify bottlenecks, and communicate design decisions. A structured framework helps candidates approach complex design problems systematically and demonstrates strong engineering thinking.
questions.explanation:
Recommended Framework:

1. Clarify Requirements
Understand functional and non-functional requirements.

2. Estimate Scale
Users, requests, storage, bandwidth.

3. Design High-Level Architecture
Major components and interactions.

4. Design Data Model
Databases, schemas, storage choices.

5. Address Scalability
Load balancing, caching, partitioning.

6. Discuss Reliability
Replication, failover, backups.

7. Identify Bottlenecks
Potential performance issues.

8. Propose Optimizations
Caching, CDNs, queues, indexing.

9. Discuss Trade-Offs
Consistency vs availability.
Cost vs performance.
Complexity vs simplicity.

10. Summarize Final Design
Review architecture and decisions.

Following this framework allows engineers to solve most system design problems in a structured and professional manner.
questions.code_example: Requirements -> Scale -> HLD -> Database -> Scalability -> Reliability -> Tradeoffs
